This presentation (thunderclap headache reaching maximum intensity within seconds, 'worst headache of my life', with vomiting, neck stiffness, and photophobia) is a classic subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH) presentation. result.verdict_summary: This presentation is a surgical emergency — do not image, refer immediately to ED for suspected testicular torsion.
met_criteria: Suspected testicular torsion — explicitly listed as 'Refer for acute assessment without initial imaging' [p57]
result.notes: The US Scrotum criteria explicitly list 'Suspected testicular torsion' under 'Refer for acute assessment — without initial imaging' [p57]. Community imaging is contraindicated. The US Abdomen criteria also list suspected testicular torsion as a redirect to acute assessment without imaging [p31].
result.safety_alert: SURGICAL EMERGENCY: Clinical findings (sudden onset severe testicular pain, high-riding testis, absent cremasteric reflex, nausea/vomiting, 3-hour onset) are classic for testicular torsion. Viability is time-critical — surgical salvage rates exceed 90% within 6 hours but fall sharply thereafter. Do NOT wait for ultrasound. Send patient to ED immediately via the fastest available means.
result.redirect: ED immediately — suspected testicular torsion is a surgical emergency. CRR community ultrasound is explicitly excluded. Ultrasound must not delay surgical assessment.

result.verdict_summary: This presentation is a surgical emergency — do not image, refer immediately to ED for suspected testicular torsion.
result.notes: Classic presentation: adolescent male, sudden severe testicular pain waking from sleep, high-riding testis, absent cremasteric reflex, nausea and vomiting. Sensitivity of ultrasound for torsion is insufficient to rule out the diagnosis and must never be used as a gatekeeper to surgery. Time to detorsion is the critical determinant of testicular salvage.
result.safety_alert: SUSPECTED TESTICULAR TORSION — SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Do not wait for imaging. Call 111 or send directly to ED immediately. Viability window is approximately 6 hours from onset; this patient is already 3 hours in. Imaging must not delay surgical exploration.
result.redirect: ED / surgical emergency — testicular torsion is explicitly excluded from community ultrasound (US Scrotum criteria, p57). Refer for acute surgical assessment without imaging.

result.verdict_summary: An eGFR of 8 (drop from baseline 62) meets the red-flag threshold for acute renal function deterioration requiring urgent secondary care assessment, not community ultrasound.
met_criteria: Acute renal function deterioration — serum creatinine/eGFR drop far exceeds threshold (eGFR 8 vs baseline 62, drop >54 units, well above the 'eGFR decrease >15 in 12 months' progressive CKD marker and consistent with acute deterioration criteria) [p53]
No catheter in situ, no obvious precipitant, not on nephrotoxics — supports genuine unexplained AKI [p53]
Reduced urine output documented — consistent with AKI presentation [p53]
result.notes: The US Renal criteria at p53 include 'Acute renal function deterioration — serum creatinine increase >50 micromol/L in 7 days or >25 micromol/L in 2 days' as an Acute 48hr criterion, with a note that discussion with secondary care/PCRL is recommended. However, an eGFR of 8 (from 62) represents end-organ failure severity that goes well beyond a community-managed acute demand scan — this patient almost certainly requires hospital admission. The CT KUB criteria also flag eGFR <45 as a red flag requiring admission rather than imaging [p26]. The redirect to secondary care takes precedence over any community imaging pathway here.
result.safety_alert: ACUTE HOSPITAL REFERRAL REQUIRED: eGFR of 8 with a baseline of 62 represents a severe, life-threatening acute kidney injury. This patient requires immediate secondary care assessment — do not defer for community imaging. Manage per Acute Kidney Injury pathway or call secondary care now. Community ultrasound is not appropriate at this stage.
result.redirect: Acute Kidney Injury pathway — refer immediately to secondary care (ED or acute medical team). Community renal ultrasound criteria note that acute renal function deterioration should be managed per the Acute Kidney Injury pathway or with secondary care specialist advice [p55]. If secondary care recommends ultrasound as part of inpatient or urgent outpatient workup, that request should come from the admitting team.
